coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis, high fluorescence and flame retardancy of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chang
Li, Hongying
Cheng, Rui
Guo, Jiazhuang
Li, Guo-Xing
Li, Qing
Wang, Cai-Feng
Yang, Xiaoning
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Facile strategy enabling the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) with favorable performance useful for various applications is highly desirable. Here, we report the fabrication of highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) towards sensitive metal ion detection, brightly fluorescent printing pattern, as well as green flame retardants for synthetic polymeric materials. CDs are synthesized by solvothermal treatment of polypropylene carbonate (PPC) with ethylenediamine, and the quantum yield of CDs could increase from 30% to 86% by further adding trace of citric acid. The abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs allow CDs well incorporated into polymers to form CD-loaded polystyrene (PS) microspheres, which are employed to construct fluorescent supraballs via triphase microfluidic technique and used as “inks” for uniform fluorescent patterns. Interestingly, the resultant CDs show good flame retardancy for highly flammable polymeric foams and fibers. The CDs surpass previously reported flame-retardant additives, to show excellent combustion resistance that the addition of 20 wt% CDs causes 75% decrease in the peak of heat release rate (P-HRR) for polyurethane (PU) foams. Significantly, a molecular dynamics simulation process for PU/CDs combustion is constructed. This work may spur the preparation and application of high-performance carbon-based nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Flame retardancy
Molecular dynamics simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Continuous production of CD-loaded PS supraballs via microfluidic technique
Generation of tiny latex droplets via inkjet printing [30]
Molecular dynamics simulation of the flame retardant behavior of CDs
Syntheses, structural features and optical characterizations of carbon dots
Integration of CDs with polymers to construct fluorescent supraballs and fluorescent patterns
Fire retardancy of carbon dots for polymeric foams and fibers

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and Characterizations of CDs. (a) Schematic solvothermal treatment of PPC with ethylenediamine yielding brown solution of CDs with bright blue fluorescence. (b) TEM image of CDs. Inset: particle size distribution of CDs. (c) HRTEM image of CDs. (d) XRD pattern of CDs. (e) XPS spectra of survey scan, and high-resolution N1s, O1s and C1s XPS spectra of CDs. (f) Raman spectrum of CDs. (g) UV-Vis absorption and PL emission spectra of CDs. (h) Fluorescence decay of CDs.

Fluorescence applications of CDs. (a) Continuous production of CD-loaded PS supraballs via microfluidic technique. (b) TEM image of CD-loaded PS microspheres. (c) Confocal microscope images of fluorescent supraballs. (d) Scheme of inkjet printing and photographs of printed patterns obtained by using CD-loaded PS microspheres as fluorescent ink.

Combustion resistance of CDs. (a–c) Photographs and combustion test of PU foams loaded with different concentrations of CDs. (d–g) Combustion process of PU/CDs-20 wt% foam. The PU/CDs-20 wt% foam did not ignite on the flame for 20 s but the stick got fire. (h) TGA curves of PU and PU/CDs-20 wt% foams measured in air with a heating rate of 40 °C min−1. (i) TG-MS curves of CDs. (j) HRR curves of PU, PU/CDs-10 wt% and PU/CDs-20 wt% foams. (k) Comparison of P-HRR reduction of different flame-retardant additives for PU foams, including Modified CNT [41], halogen and composite additives [42], polydopamine [43], Multi-layered MMT/CNT [44] and CDs in this case. (l) The mechanism of flame retardancy of CDs for polymeric foams and fibers.

(a) SEM images of PU/CDs and (b) PAN/CDs nanofibers films. (c) HRR curves of PU/CDs nanofiber films with different concentrations of CDs. (d) HRR curves of PAN/CDs nanofiber films with different concentrations of CDs.

Combustion process of CDs/PU from MD simulation. Unit structure of (a) CDs and (b) PU. The colors represent different atoms as follows; white: H, red: O, green: C, and blue: N. Combustion simulation process for (c) CDs combustion, (d) PU combustion and (e) PU/CDs combustion. The center panel (c-e): the weight proportion of the four types products after combustion.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis, high fluorescence and flame retardancy of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile strategy enabling the fabrication of carbon dots (CDs) with favorable performance useful for various applications is highly desirable. Here, we report the fabrication of highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) towards sensitive metal ion detection, brightly fluorescent printing pattern, as well as green flame retardants for synthetic polymeric materials. CDs are synthesized by solvothermal treatment of polypropylene carbonate (PPC) with ethylenediamine, and the quantum yield of CDs could increase from 30% to 86% by further adding trace of citric acid. The abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs allow CDs well incorporated into polymers to form CD-loaded polystyrene (PS) microspheres, which are employed to construct fluorescent supraballs via triphase microfluidic technique and used as “inks” for uniform fluorescent patterns. Interestingly, the resultant CDs show good flame retardancy for highly flammable polymeric foams and fibers. The CDs surpass previously reported flame-retardant additives, to show excellent combustion resistance that the addition of 20 wt% CDs causes 75% decrease in the peak of heat release rate (P-HRR) for polyurethane (PU) foams. Significantly, a molecular dynamics simulation process for PU/CDs combustion is constructed. This work may spur the preparation and application of high-performance carbon-based nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a unique class of nanoparticles derived from carbon source precursors, have received increasing attention owing to their favorable attributes, such as low-cost preparation, high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY), good dispersibility in water and organic solvents, good biocompatibility, or low toxicity [1]. Such nanomaterials show promising potentials in a variety of applications such as bioimaging, nanomedicine, sensing, or optoelectronic devices [2–4]. To date, great progress has been made in the development of CDs. For instance, the high fluorescence efficiency of CDs has been achieved, with PL QY over 80%, posing the potential of CDs for use in lighting and display technologies [5,6]. The high surface functionalization makes CDs responsive to external stimuli, and hence the fluorescence sensing of CDs toward metal ions such as Fe3+, Cu2+, or Co2+ have been well investigated [7,8]. The application of CDs as “ink” for printing has also been demonstrated to yield fluorescent patterns, which foreshadow the potentials of CDs for printing optical units of optoelectronic devices, fluorescent coding and anti-counterfeiting [9,10]. Despite great advances in this area, much work is still highly needed, such as exploring new synthetic strategies for preparing CDs with high PL QY, suppressing ubiquitous coffee ring effect [11] and aggregation-induced PL quenching [12] upon film forming to print uniform and brightly fluorescent patterns, or exploiting more extensive applications.

On the other hand, synthetic polymeric materials are increasingly replacing metals and wood to be widely used in industry, commerce, agriculture and household applications in modern society. As these synthetic polymers are highly combustible, especially these possessing foam and fiber structures with large surface area, modifications to improve their combustion resistance are required. As such, flame retardants become one of the largest chemical additives in plastics industry today [13–15]. In this respect, flame-retardant additives containing halogens (brominated and chlorinated compounds), phosphorus, and nitrogen have been successively developed, respectively [16]. Unfortunately, currently commercial halogenated, phosphorous or composite additives pose a great hazard to the environment and human health, and emit toxic smoke during burning threatening human life [17–21]. Recent work also shows the promising combustion resistance of inorganic nanofillers towards polymers, like carbon nanotubes [22], Al(OH)3 [23], and Mg(OH)2 [24], etc. Very recently, Shariatinia's group demonstrated that nitrogen and phosphorous-doped carbon-based nanomaterials could be useful to reduce the flammability of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) [25]. In this regard, much work is further needed. The mechanism of flame retardancy of CDs for polymers needs to be further revealed. The combustion resistance behavior of CDs towards extremely flammable polymeric foams and fibers has yet to be investigated. The universal application of CDs for flame retardants of polymers needs to be assessed.

Herein, we report the new synthesis of CDs with high PL QY, and their potentials for use as “ink” for printing highly fluorescent patterns and as green flame retardants for highly flammable polymer foams and fibers. The carbonization of CO2-derived polypropylene carbonate (PPC) with ethylenediamine via solvothermal treatment forms nano-sized CDs. Proper modification of the solvothermal process by further adding trace of citric acid could yield CDs with PL QY up to 86%. Upon incorporation of CDs into polystyrene (PS) microspheres, highly fluorescent supraballs and patterns are available. Perhaps more interestingly, we make best of the as-prepared CDs to serve as green flame retardants for PU foams and fibers, and PAN fibers. As we know, polymeric foams and fibers are much more combustible than common synthetic polymers, ascribed to their large surface area. Here we study the combustion resistance of polymeric foams and fibers upon incorporation of different concentration of CDs, and the flame retardancy mechanism is proposed. Significantly, the flame retardancy of CDs is confirmed by reactive force field molecular dynamics simulation, which has never been reported before. This finding paves a promising route for fabrication of high-performance CDs with robust application prospect.

Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%), ethanol (99.7%), ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.8%) and anhydrous citric acid (CA, 99.6%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Propylene carbonate (PPC) synthesized from carbon dioxide is analytical grade and commercially available. Alcohol soluble polyurethane (PU, 45 wt% in ethanol) was purchased from Qiancheng technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water with the resistivity greater than 18 MΩ cm−1 was used in the experiments. All the reagents were used as received.

PPC (500 mg) was dissolved in DMF (12 mL), and then EDA (0.5 mL) was added and mixed uniformly. Then the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL), and then heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature to ensure safe opening. The resulting products were then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was further filtrated by ultra-filtration membrane (0.22 μm) four times to remove impurities. Then solid CDs could be obtained from the solution via vacuum rotatory evaporation (yield ca. 60%), which were used directly for further application study. For characterization on the structure and optical properties of CDs, the products were dialyzed for 3 days in a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 D) placed in deionized water.

Proper modification of the solvothermal process by further adding trace of citric acid could greatly improve the QY of CDs to 86%. Specifically, PPC (500 mg) and EDA (0.5 mL) in DMF (12 mL) was heated to 60 °C at which citric acid (0.2 mg) was quickly added. Then the obtained mixture was treated with the solvothermal process and purification mentioned above.

Monodispersed PS microspheres functionalized with carboxyl groups were prepared via seed emulsion polymerization according to our previous works [26,27]. First of all, 0.24 g of PVP, 0.3 g of NaHCO3 and 100 g of ultra-pure water mixed uniformly via ultrasonic device and poured into the four-necked flask. After the mixed solution was stirred for half an hour under a nitrogen atmosphere at 98 °C, 5 g of St was added. Then, the solution composed of 15 g of ultra-pure water and 0.04 g of KPS was added the four-necked flask via a microfluidic syringe pump to touch off polymerization. 0.3 g of BA and 0.3 g of AA were added into the flask after 1.5 h. Meanwhile, the mixture water of 0.1 g of KPS and 10 g of water was added at a steady speed. P(St-BA-AA) colloidal polymer nanoparticles were obtained after three hours of emulsion polymerization reaction. The solution was removed the unreacted filter residue and centrifuged to increase the concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Continuous production of CD-loaded PS supraballs via microfluidic technique
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent hybrid microbeads were fabricated via chips and microfluidic technology [28,29]. We chose monodispersed PS (200 nm) via emulsion polymerization as cores load CDs obtained above to synthesize CD-loaded PS monodispersed supraballs. The microfluidic device was assembled by inserting a capillary tube with a Y-shaped chip platform into a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) tube. Two discontinuous phase (CDs solution and polystyrene emulsion) and the continuous phase (methyl silicone oil) were injected into PDMS tube, respectively, by syringe pumps at independently adjusted flow velocities. One of the discontinuous flowing phases was the as-prepared polystyrene emulsion, the emulsion concentration was 20 wt%, and the other one was the 0.1 wt% CD solution. The two discontinuous streams formed a pair of droplets, and then were immediately incorporated into a larger single channel where the continuous phase was methyl silicone oil, and then were transferred to a container. The CD-loaded PS supraballs were obtained after evaporation at 70 °C for 8 h. Finally, the supraballs were washed with ethanol to remove residual methyl silicone oil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of tiny latex droplets via inkjet printing [30]
ce:section.ce:para: A commercially available inkjet printer (Jetlab 4, from MicroFab Corp., USA) was used for all the inkjet printing trials. The printer has two stroboscopic cameras: one is to observe the droplet formation from the nozzle, and the other mounted vertically with respect to the surface of the substrate holder, to observe the deposited ink droplets. The printer is capable of independent X, Y and Z-stage movement: the first two were to position the substrate holder, colloid deposits were printed by using piezo-actuated nozzle with inner diameters of 30, 50 and 70 μm. The substrate holder and the print-head could be independently heated to control the ink drying temperature. Silicon wafers, glasses, or plastic films were used as the printing substrates. Herein, the size of the colloid deposits was associated with many factors including the nozzle diameters, surface properties of the printing substrates, the distance between the printhead and substrates, and printing parameters (applied voltage and frequency). The CD-loaded PS microspheres were printed on the paper substrate to provide various fluorescent patterns. As the inject-printing was completed, stable fluorescent patterns appeared in 2–3 s.

Varying amounts of CDs were soak-loaded onto PU foams, dried at 80 °C, to obtain PU/CDs foams with different content of CDs (5 wt%, 10 wt%, and 20 wt%, respectively). PU/CDs nanofibers obtained by solution blow spinning : The polymeric solution systems for blow spinning contained 30 wt% PU and certain amount of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamics simulation of the flame retardant behavior of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: AMS was used to perform all the ReaxFF MD simulations with the periodic boundary condition applied in three directions. H/C/O/N/S/B force field parameters were used in the present study. Then a model of CDs coated with PU was built, put into a 60*60*60 Angstrom box, and the Minimization method was optimized first, then 25 ps NPT (temperature 273 K, pressure 0.1 MPa). Subsequently, a 5 ps NVT (constant number, constant volume and constant temperature) was performed (the system was kept in a Berendsen thermostat with a damping constant of 100 fs to perform an NVT MD simulation). This part of the processing was used to simulate the actual state of matter and make the entire system in equilibrium. Finally, put the polymer processed in step 2 into a 200*200*200 Angstrom box, and put 1200 oxygen molecules (the stoichiometric ratio of CDs to O2 is 0.5) at the same time, reacting for 150 ps at a temperature of 3000 K.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of CDs were performed by a JEOL JEM-2100 electron microscope. A Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer were employed to provide Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. A Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10 infrared microscope was used for IR images, which was equipped with a liquid-nitrogen-cooled MCT detector (Thermo Electron Corporation). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Raman spectrum were recorded on a Bruker AXS D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer, an ES-CAIAB250 XPS system, and a Horiba HR 800 Raman system, respectively. UV−vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 900 UV−vis spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) was performed with a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer. Absolute quantum yield was measured on Edinburgh FLS980. Fluorescence microscopy images were measured by a laser scanning confocal microscope.

Fluorescence decay was measured on an Edinburgh FLS980 system, fitted by biexponential function Y(t) using Eq. (1)

are fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetimes τ1, τ2 [31,32]. The average lifetime τ was deduced from Eq. (2)

α 1

τ 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses, structural features and optical characterizations of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The one-step solvothermal treatment of PPC with ethylenediamine induces decomposition and surface passivation, yielding CDs with high yield of 60 wt% (Fig. 1 (a); see experimental details). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image shows the CDs are well-dispersed spherical particles with an average size of 3.2 nm (Fig. 1(b)). Lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.22 ± 0.01 nm were observed, corresponding to (100) diffraction facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 1(c)) [33]. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern displays a broad peak indexed to (002), suggesting small size and partial graphitization of CDs (Fig. 1(d)) [34]. To further reveal the chemical identity and functional groups for CDs, we performed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. Full-scan survey of XPS proves the composition of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements in CDs (Fig. 1(e)), which shows C 1s, N 1s and O 1s signals at 285, 400 and 532 eV, respectively. The high-resolution N1s, O1s and C1s XPS spectra suggest the existence of amino N pyridine-like C−N, N−(C)3, C=O, C=C, and C−OH functional groups [35–37], which are confirmed by FT- IR spectra (Fig. S1). Raman spectrum of CDs shows the G band at 1640 cm−1 and the D band at 1310 cm−1 (Fig. 1(f)), revealing the presence of both graphitic sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms in CDs. The above results indicate the carbonization of PPC into CDs composed of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms with rich peripheral functional groups and sp3 carbon defects [38,39].

The optical properties of CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1(g), CDs show an absorption peak at around 351 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CDs show characteristic excitation-dependent behavior (Fig. S2). As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission position red-shifts. The CDs present PL peak located at 520 nm under excitation at 440 nm, and the absolute PL QY was determined to be 30%. Besides, PL decay dynamics indicates lifetime of τ = 8.3 ns for CDs powder (Fig. 1(h)). Proper modification on the solvothermal process by further adding trace of citric acid could greatly improve the PL QY of CDs. The triple detection size exclusion chromatography (TD-SEC) analysis shows that both kinds of CDs have extreme narrow molecular weight distribution (PDI = 1.05 and 1.04, respectively), and the unimodal curve indicates pure component of CDs in both cases (Fig. S3). The resulting CDs have similar spherical morphology with an average size of 2.9 nm (Fig. S4). Smaller size corresponds to the blue-shift of PL peak (Fig. S2, S5). And they show PL emission at 460 nm with absolute PL QY up to 86% under excitation at 400 nm (Fig. S5), along with prolonged PL lifetime (τ = 13.8 ns, Fig. S6). Raman spectra indicate higher intensity ratio of the two bands (ID/IG) in CDs with 86% of PL QY (Figs. 1(f) and S7), revealing these CDs have higher percentage of sp3-hybridized carbon. The smaller size, decreased graphitization degree and richer surface functionalization might be responsible for the blue-shift of PL emission, improved PL QY and prolonged PL lifetime [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of CDs with polymers to construct fluorescent supraballs and fluorescent patterns
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the good surface functionalization and optical properties, the CDs show various promising potentials taking advantage of their fluorescence, such as metal ion sensors, integration of CDs and polymers to fabricate fluorescent nanocomposites, and fluorescent inks. The abundant surface functional groups not only facilitate fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions (Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Li+) at 1.00 × 10−9 M level (Fig. S8), but also allow the CDs to show good compatibility with polymer matrix. Accordingly, the CDs could be well incorporated into polymers to obtain highly fluorescent namocomposites. We realized continuous production of CD-loaded polystyrene (PS) supraballs via triphase microfluidic technique (Fig. 2(a)). Here, monodispersed PS microspheres (200 nm) loaded with CDs were formed and served as building blocks for assembly of fluorescent supraballs with tunable size (100–500 μm). Because the PS microspheres are surface-functionalized with carboxyl groups and the surface of CDs contains abundant −OH, C=O and −NH functional groups (Fig. S1), there are efficient hydrogen bonds between PS microspheres and nanoparticles CDs. As such, CDs are well-dispersed on the PS surface (Fig. 2(b)), circumventing PL quenching problems derived from aggregation of CDs [12], to maintain good fluorescence in the resultant nanocomposite supraballs. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the supraballs exhibit different bright colors under different wavelengths of excitation light, retaining the excitation-dependent PL characteristics of CDs and uniform distribution of CDs on PS surface.

Furthermore, the CD-loaded PS microspheres could be used as “inks” for luminescent patterns (Fig. 2(d)). The printing processes are normally accompanied with the ubiquitous nature of the coffee ring effect [11,30], leading to limited pattern resolution. In this case, the CDs contain rich surface functional groups, providing plenty of binding sites to couple with surface-functinalized PS. Moreover, CD-loaded PS microspheres have a relatively rough surface structure compared with pure PS microspheres, which can deform the air-water interface and form a retained structure, causing the opposite Marangoni flow, so as to alleviate the solution flow from center to the edge. As a result, the assembly of CDs/PS particles can effectively inhibit the formation of coffee rings. It is worth noting that the fluorescent patterns constructed from these CD-loaded PS microspheres maintain after 2-year storage under indoor conditions. Therefore, with use of CD-loaded PS microspheres as printing inks, uniform and brightly fluorescent patterns could be obtained, which might be useful for practical applications such as optoelectronic devices, sensing, fluorescent coding and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fire retardancy of carbon dots for polymeric foams and fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Then we exploited the application of CDs as green flame retardants for highly flammable polymer foams and fibers. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs show excellent combustion resistance, and surpass previously reported flame-retardant additives to effectively decrease the flammability of polyurethane (PU) foams (Fig. 3). Varying amounts of CDs were soak-loaded onto PU foams (Fig. S9), dried at 80 °C, to obtain PU/CDs foams with different content of CDs (5 wt%, 10 wt%, and 20 wt%, respectively). The combustion of the pure and composite foams on flame was investigated. Pristine PU foam immediately and completely burned upon exposure to the flame, and completely lost the original shape (Fig. S10). For the PU/CDs-5 wt% foam, the burning and great shrink of the foam were observed on the flame for 5 s (s) (Fig. 3 (a)). As the content of CDs increases, PU foams exhibit better flame retardancy (Fig. 3(b, c)). In particular, the PU/CDs-20 wt% foam did not ignite on the flame for 20 s, and hence maintained material shape after burning test (Fig. 3(d–g)). We noted the sample gradually lost its fluorescence derived from CDs during burning, and formed a continuous protective solid layer on the burning surface (Fig. S11). The presence of the protective surface layer is believed to be crucial for the flammability reduction [40].

Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) indicates that the PU/CDs-20 wt% foam shows a higher decomposition temperature compared with pristine PU (Fig. 3(h)). The PU/CDs-20 wt% foam shows the first decomposition at 242 °C, which is much higher than that of PU at 140 °C. This feature suggests that the initial decomposition temperature of the PU/CDs-20 wt% foam is nearly 100 °C higher than that of pure PU foam, indicating that PU/CDs has better thermal stability. At 700 °C, the amount of residue of PU/CDs-20 wt% is 8 wt% while that for PU is only 2 wt%, indicating higher residue in the CD-loaded PU foam. The higher residue and thermal stability in PU/CDs foams may be related to the formation of CD-derived char layer, consistent with the formation of continuous protective solid layer during the combustion test mentioned above (Fig. S11). We further utilized the thermo gravimetric mass (TG-MS) spectrometry to analyze the behavior of CDs in air atmosphere at high temperature (Fig. 3(i)). The CDs show decomposition at 160 °C, and an amount of carbon residue of 36% at 500 °C. There are mainly two major downward trends in TG curve of CDs, one between 175 and 225 °C and the other between 325 and 400 °C. The mass spectrometry indicates the weight loss in these two temperature ranges is attributed to the conversion of CDs into CO2. This feature suggests a non-flammable gas of CO2 could form during combustion. Therefore, we propose that double protective layers, i.e. a non-flammable CO2 gas layer and a continuous char solid layer, form on the surface of the PU foams during the combustion process of PU/CDs foams on ethanol flame (Fig. 3(l)). These double protective layers play as an oxygen barrier on the PU surface, and hence effectively reduce the flammability of the polymer materials.

To further assess the flame retardancy of CDs, we carried out oxygen consumption cone calorimetry. Fig. 3(j) shows the heat release rate (HRR) curves for pristine PU foam and PU/CDs foams. The HRR curve for pristine PU foam consists of two peaks, the first peak attributed to the decomposition of isocyanate and the other ascribed to the pyrolysis of polyol. Whereas, one more peak appears at early stage of pyrolysis combustion for PU/CDs foams, assigned to the decomposition of CDs. Interestingly, the PU/CDs foams have much lower peaks of HRR (P-HRR); the introduction of 20 wt% of CDs into PU foams causes 75% decrease in P-HRR. The results further confirm excellent combustion resistance of CDs for PU foams. To our knowledge, CDs induce highly efficient reduction in P-HRR (75%) for PU foams compared with commercial or literature-reported flame retardant additives, such as halogen additive (37%), composite additive (43%), modified carbon nanotubes (Modified CNT, 35%), polydopamine (67%), and multi-layered montmorillonite/carbon nanotubes (Multi-layered MMT/CNT, 69%), as shown in Fig. 3(k) and Table S1, suggesting the superior flame retardancy of CDs. Besides, traditional halogenated or composite additives generate toxic gases themselves during burning such as HBr or HCl. In this case, however, the combustion of CDs mainly produces nontoxic and nonflammable CO2 as mentioned above (Fig. 3(i)). Therefore, the CDs developed in this case are easy-prepared and low-toxic, and show superior flame retardancy, which might be alternative flame-retardant additives to replace traditional harmful halogen and halogen-free flame retardants.

We further assessed the universal application of CDs for flame retardants of polymers (Fig. 4). PU/CDs fiber films with average diameter of 700 nm were fabricated (Fig. 4 (a)). A micro combustion calorimetry was used to investigate the effect of CDs on the flame retardancy in nanofiber films. The HRR curves are shown in Fig. 4(c). For PU/CDs-10 wt%, the P-HRR is decreased by 41.2%. Besides, comparing to pure PU nanofiber films, the heat release capacity (HRC) and total heat release (THR) of PU/CDs-10 wt% are reduced by 41.9% and 24.7%, respectively (Supplementary Table 2). Meanwhile, the combustion resistance of PAN/CDs fiber films were investigated (Fig. 4(b, d)). For PAN/CDs-10 wt%, HRC, P-HRR and THR are reduced by 42.5%, 42.7% and 23.9%, respectively (Fig. 4(d)). We also investigated the combustion behavior of PU foams and PAN nanofibers with incorporation of CDs derived from other precursors (e.g. citric acid and urea, Fig. S12), which was prepared via a large-scale production strategy we developed previously [45], to address the cost problem of using CDs as flame retardants. For PAN/CDs-10 wt% nanofibers films, HRC, P-HRR and THR are reduced by 38.6%, 34.7% and 20.9%, and for PU/CDs-10 wt% nanofibers films, HRC, P-HRR and THR are reduced by 40.1%, 38.8% and 23.0%, respectively (Fig. S12). Besides, it has been reported that CDs could be used as nanofillers to enhance the mechanical properties of polymer materials [46]. Therefore, the results imply that CDs could be used universally as flame-retardant additives for polymeric materials.

Finally, molecular dynamics simulation was conducted to theoretically demonstrate the behavior of flame retardancy of CDs. Herein, the reactive force field (ReaxFF) [47] was used to describe the combustion reaction, wherein the bond breakage and bond formation are treated based on the bond orders from the interatomic distances. In this simulation, the molecular models of CDs and PU were constructed from the repeated unit structures [48], respectively, as shown in Fig. 5 (a, b). The obtained molecular masses for the CDs and PU are 4322 and 9540, respectively, which are close to the measured molecular masses of the CDs (4292) and PU (10399) chains by size exclusion chromatography (Figs. S3a and S13).

All MD simulations were performed in the NVT ensemble in the Lammps code [49]. Before the dynamics simulations, the CDs, the PU, and the combined complex of CDs and PU were optimized. The optimized structures (in the left panels) for the three systems show very intricate network feature due to the establishment of both intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Fig. 5(c) (left panel) shows the configuration formed by eight CDs. Fig. 5(d) shows the configuration formed by two long PU chains. The complex of CDs and PU (Fig. 5(e)) is formed by eight CDs and two long PU chains. After the combustion simulation under 3000 K, as shown in Fig. 5(c–e), it is observed there are different configurations for the three systems. For the sake of simplicity, the final compounds after combustion process were divided into four classes based on the number of carbon atoms [50]. The fragments with <4 carbon atoms are regarded as gas. The fragments with C5-C13 are regarded as light tar and the fragments with C14-C40 are regarded as heavy tar of C14-C40. The fragments with >40 carbon atoms are regarded as char.

For CDs (Fig. 5(c)), as expected, no obvious structure breakage occurs during the combustion process, consistent with its flame retardancy function. More than 92% of the carbon atoms are in form of char after combustion, indicating that CDs is non-combustion. However, for PU, the long chain molecules were combusted into the small molecules, corresponding to the complete combustion. Analysis of the combustion products shows that 54.6% of the carbon atoms are in form of gas, and the other are oil. However, in Fig. 5(e), the complex of CDs and PU shows stable structure during the combustion. Most of the remaining species is in the form of the char together with small fraction of heavy tar during the combustion under 3000 K. Through the reactive force field, we found that the CD-derived char layer could exist at a temperature of 3000 K (Fig. S14). This might provide a theoretical support that the morphology of the PU can be effectively kept by combing the CDs during the combustion process.

Our findings constitute a new nanomaterials synthesis strategy to prepare carbon dots (CDs) with high photoluminescence quantum yield, and demonstrate the applications of CDs as “ink” for printing highly fluorescent patterns and as green flame retardants for highly flammable materials including polymeric foams and fibers. The carbonization of polypropylene carbonate with ethylenediamine via solvothermal treatment forms brightly fluorescent CDs, and the quantum yield cloud be improved to 86% by further adding trace of citric acid. Highly fluorescent supraballs and patterns could be prepared upon incorporation of the resultant CDs into polystyrene (PS) microspheres. More importantly, specific examples indicate that the resultant low-toxic CDs surpass previously reported flame-retardant additives to show excellent combustion resistance for polyurethane foams. Moreover, unlike traditional halogenated or composite additives generating toxic gases themselves during burning, the combustion of CDs mainly produces nontoxic and nonflammable CO2. We also construct a molecular dynamics simulation process for polyurethane/CDs combustion. The CDs developed in this work are easy-prepared and low-toxic, and show superior flame retardancy, which might be alternative flame-retardant additives for various synthetic polymeric materials and even wood. Thus, this work paves a promising approach for fabrication of high-performance CDs with various robust applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0401700, 2018YFC1602800), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21736006), Fund of State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201704, ZK201716), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2021.06.056.
